title: Managing Montreux: Turkey and the Russia-Ukraine War in the Black Sea
gist: Carnegie Europe's Thomas de Waal argues Turkey's 1936 Montreux Convention powers have made Ankara the indispensable gatekeeper of Black Sea security since 2022, letting it simultaneously aid Ukraine's naval survival and pursue 'managed rivalry' with Russia; he concludes Ukraine's Western partners have no option but to work within Turkey's framework and lists concrete levers (oil-carrier inspections, Bulgaria/Romania capacity-building, a Turkish-led ceasefire force) to make that engagement count.

insights.claim: Four days after Russia's February 2022 invasion, Turkey invoked its 1936 Montreux Convention authority to restrict warship passage through the Turkish Straits, a move that tilted the early military balance in the Black Sea and, combined with verbal side-agreements with Russia and NATO, helped Ukraine repel Russian naval attacks on Odesa.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Establishes the Montreux Convention as a load-bearing, still-active lever in the Russia-Ukraine war — any escalation or ceasefire scenario in the Black Sea has to route through Turkish decision-making, not just Kyiv/Moscow/Washington.
insights.locator: On February 28, 2022, four days after Russia’s full-scale invasion

insights.claim: Since over 90% of Ukraine's agricultural exports moved through Black Sea ports before the war, Ukraine's ability to keep its three Odesa-region ports open — enabled significantly by Turkey's Montreux restrictions on Russian naval reinforcement — is described as vital to the country's survival.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Quantifies just how central Black Sea port access is to Ukraine's economic survival, making Turkish strait policy a first-order variable for Ukrainian grain-export and broader economic-resilience forecasts.
insights.locator: As more than 90 percent of Ukraine’s agricultural exports

insights.claim: Turkey's Black Sea strategy rests on three principles: 'regional ownership' by the littoral states (discouraging broader NATO naval presence), de-escalation (the 2022 'Montreux Plus' verbal deals with Russia and NATO), and freedom of civilian navigation, which Turkey contrasts with Iran's disruption of the Strait of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: A clear three-part framework for predicting Turkish behavior in any future Black Sea crisis — Ankara will keep favoring littoral-only naval control and resist wider NATO involvement even as it supports Ukraine.
insights.quote: We called on everyone to avoid further militarization of the Black Sea maritime zone
insights.locator: characterized as “Montreux Plus” by one Turkish official

insights.claim: Under the 2022 verbal 'Montreux Plus' arrangement, Russia agreed not to return Black Sea Fleet warships to their home bases in exchange for non-littoral NATO states staying out of the straits; Turkish officials estimate up to thirty Russian warships stationed elsewhere could have exercised their Article 21 right to return but were refused entry when Russia formally requested sending four warships in.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of naval reinforcement Turkey has kept out of the Black Sea (up to 30 vessels) — a concrete measure of how much Turkish policy has capped Russia's regional naval capacity throughout the war.
insights.locator: Under Article 21 of the Montreux Convention, Russia had the right

insights.claim: Turkey's current straits restrictions will persist as long as the war continues; a Turkish official said a ceasefire alone will not change Ankara's stance and that only a durable truce or actual settlement would qualify as peace sufficient to lift the restrictions.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Sets a clear, falsifiable marker for when the wartime Montreux regime will end — a mere ceasefire announcement should not be read by markets as triggering an immediate reopening of Black Sea shipping/naval dynamics.
insights.locator: The Turkish position is that only a durable truce

insights.claim: The Montreux Convention has proven extremely durable for ninety years — surviving a direct challenge when Stalin demanded it be scrapped after WWII (which instead drove Turkey into NATO in 1952) — even though much of its language is archaic, referencing dispute resolution by the defunct League of Nations, and Turkey secured a carve-out preserving the regime under UNCLOS despite never signing that treaty.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: The convention's near-immunity to renegotiation (never amended despite formal provisions allowing it) suggests it will remain the operative legal framework for Black Sea security indefinitely, rather than being replaced by a modernized regime.
insights.quote: a situation in which Turkey had a hand on Russia’s throat
insights.locator: the Soviet leader, Joseph Stalin, complained

insights.claim: Turkish President Erdoğan's proposed Istanbul Canal — a bypass to the Bosporus that would let Turkey charge shipping tolls and sidestep Montreux tonnage/warship rules — remains official policy but has been suspended, primarily for economic rather than political reasons, per Turkey's transportation minister in 2025.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: A dormant but not dead structural threat to the Montreux regime — if Turkey ever revives and completes the Istanbul Canal, it could unilaterally alter Black Sea naval access rules outside the convention's constraints, a low-probability but high-impact watch item.
insights.locator: when in 2011 Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan announced plans

themes.theme: Managed rivalry: Turkey's balancing act with Russia

insights.claim: Turkey and Russia practice what Bilkent University's Seçkin Köstem calls 'managed rivalry' in the Black Sea — combining historic contestation with a shared preference for minimal Western interference and mutually beneficial trade, which has boomed since 2022 as Turkish Airlines became the largest international carrier operating in Russia.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Names the operative bilateral framework analysts should use for Turkey-Russia relations — not alliance, not confrontation, but a durable transactional coexistence that will likely outlast the war.
insights.locator: which Seçkin Köstem of Bilkent University has called

insights.claim: Turkey's posture is 'pro-Ukraine but not anti-Russia,' per Kadir Has University's Mustafa Aydin — a difficult stance in practice reflected in Ankara's continued tolerance of Russian 'shadow fleet' vessels transiting the straits, including some carrying grain seized from occupied Ukraine or oil bound for Turkish refineries.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Turkey's tacit tolerance of Russia's shadow fleet is a live sanctions-evasion channel through the Bosporus — relevant for anyone tracking the effectiveness of Western oil-price-cap and shadow-fleet enforcement.
insights.locator: in the current war, said Mustafa Aydin of Kadir Has University

insights.claim: Ship-tracking expert Yörük Işık estimates roughly one-fifth of the five-to-fifteen oil carriers transiting the straits daily is a Russian shadow-fleet vessel, and while there were reports of Russia using civilian roll-on-roll-off auxiliary vessels to smuggle weapons through the straits in violation of Montreux, Işık says Turkey appears to have quietly stopped tolerating this specific practice roughly a year before the article's publication.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, sourced estimate (roughly 20% of daily oil-carrier traffic) for shadow-fleet volume through the straits — directly useful for modeling how much sanctioned Russian oil is still moving via this route.
insights.quote: There was open violation by roll-on-roll-off civilian auxiliary vessels. We can assume that Turkey had intelligence on this and preferred to ignore suspicious activities. But it has stopped—we haven’t seen this for one year now

insights.claim: Russian officials have repeatedly signaled they will only tolerate Turkey's Montreux enforcement so long as it also keeps NATO's non-littoral members out — a 2023 joint article by five Russian authors warned Russia could invoke Article 19 against belligerents if NATO allies pressured Turkey, and former Security Council head Nikolai Patrushev said in November 2024 that Russia 'won't tolerate' permanent non-Black Sea naval presence.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific trigger (Western pressure on Turkey to admit non-littoral NATO warships) that Russia has flagged as grounds to abandon its side of the Montreux Plus deal — a concrete tripwire for any Western policy debate on expanding Black Sea naval presence.
insights.quote: Westerners should firmly understand that Russia is strongly established in the Black Sea, and we won’t allow our positions to be weakened in this region. What’s more, we won’t tolerate the permanent naval military presence of non–Black Sea states in contravention of the Montreux Convention.
insights.locator: Nikolai Patrushev, a close ally of Putin

themes.theme: Where Turkey's support for Ukraine falls short

insights.claim: In January 2024, Turkey refused passage to two UK-donated decommissioned mine hunters for Ukraine, citing Montreux's Article 19 ban on belligerents' warships transiting the straits, leaving the ships (plus two more from Belgium and the Netherlands) stranded in Portsmouth despite several hundred sea mines having drifted dangerously across Black Sea shipping lanes since 2022.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: A concrete, ongoing instance of Turkey's legalistic Montreux enforcement actively obstructing Western military aid to Ukraine — illustrates the real operational cost of Ankara's gatekeeper role for Ukraine's mine-clearing capacity.
insights.locator: in January 2024, Turkey refused these two vessels passage

insights.claim: Ukrainian officials are frustrated that Turkey extends the same Montreux-derived transit rights to Russian shadow-fleet vessels as to legitimate Ukrainian shipping facing Russian attacks and mines, with Ukrainian Navy spokesman Dmytro Ryzhenko saying ships should be able to sail directly from Odesa to the Bosphorus rather than hugging neighboring countries' territorial waters for safety.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Highlights a specific Ukrainian grievance (equal treatment of adversary shadow-fleet ships) that is a source of friction in the Turkey-Ukraine relationship despite Ankara's overall supportive posture.
insights.quote: It needs to be possible to go directly from Odesa to the Bosphorus across the middle of the Black Sea and not [hide] in the territorial waters of neighboring countries.
insights.locator: Ukrainian Navy Spokesman Dmytro Ryzhenko said in April 2025

themes.theme: Forward-looking levers for Ukraine's Western partners

insights.claim: The author's core recommendation is that Ukraine's Western partners have no realistic alternative to close engagement with Turkey on Black Sea security, since Ankara controls the legal chokepoint (the straits) and jealously guards its gatekeeper role — meaning aid strategies must be designed to align with, not bypass, Turkish interests.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: The central actionable conclusion: any Western Black Sea security initiative (demining, naval capacity-building, ceasefire enforcement) that doesn't route through or defer to Turkish leadership is likely to fail or be blocked, as happened with the UK mine hunters.
insights.locator: Given these constraints, Ukraine’s Western partners have no alternative

insights.claim: One concrete lever available to Ankara is inspecting the roughly one-in-five daily oil carriers that are Russian shadow-fleet vessels under Montreux Article 3's sanitary-inspection provision, since many of these ships are old and unseaworthy and inspection would serve Turkey's own environmental interest in the narrow, city-transiting straits.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, legally available (Article 3) and self-interested (environmental risk in a 700-meter-wide urban strait) mechanism Turkey could be persuaded to use against the Russian shadow fleet — a concrete policy ask for Western diplomats.
insights.locator: Many of these vessels are old and unseaworthy

insights.claim: A second lever is boosting the naval capacity of the two EU/NATO littoral states, Bulgaria and Romania — whose navies are roughly one-sixth the size of Turkey's (Bulgaria's even smaller) — through more training, resources, and an EU-funded maritime hub at Constanța, which fits within Turkey's 'regional ownership' doctrine rather than conflicting with it.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: A politically feasible path to strengthen NATO's Black Sea naval presence that works within, rather than against, Turkey's insistence on littoral-state primacy — a template for how the EU could fund naval buildup without triggering a Montreux dispute.
insights.locator: A second way in which Western powers can both help Ukraine

insights.claim: A third priority is supporting Turkey's already-forming ambition to lead the maritime component of a post-ceasefire coalition force for Ukraine, using its Montreux-derived guardian role to conduct patrols, air surveillance, and ceasefire enforcement in the Black Sea, building on the April 2026 Maritime Component Command and Black Sea Task Force it established with Romania and Bulgaria.
insights.speaker: Thomas de Waal
insights.so_what: Signals where the post-ceasefire security architecture for the Black Sea is already heading — a Turkish-led, littoral-state naval enforcement structure — relevant to positioning for any future Ukraine-Russia settlement negotiations.
insights.locator: a third priority is to support and encourage Turkey’s ambition
